attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of urinary incontinence. She reports leaking urine both when coughing or lifting heavy objects, and also experiences sudden urges to urinate that she cannot always control. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Abdominal examination reveals no masses or tenderness. A urinalysis is negative for infection. The GP decides that imaging is warranted as part of the initial workup.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial imaging investigation?
options: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the pelvis
Computed tomography (CT) of the abdomen and pelvis
Ultrasound of the bladder and pelvis
attributes.explanation:
In this case of a 72-year-old woman presenting with symptoms suggestive of mixed (stress and urge) urinary incontinence, the most appropriate initial imaging investigation is an ultrasound of the bladder and pelvis. Ultrasound is non-invasive, readily available, and provides valuable information about the urinary tract without exposing the patient to ionising radiation.

Ultrasound can assess bladder wall thickness, detect bladder diverticula, evaluate for pelvic organ prolapse, and most importantly, measure post-void residual volume. This last measurement is crucial in the initial evaluation of urinary incontinence, as it can help differentiate between various types of incontinence and identify potential causes such as bladder outlet obstruction or detrusor underactivity.

Computed tomography (CT) of the abdomen and pelvis is not typically used as an initial imaging study for uncomplicated urinary incontinence. While it can provide detailed anatomical information, it exposes the patient to significant radiation and is generally reserved for cases where there is suspicion of more complex urological pathology or when ultrasound results are inconclusive.

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the pelvis can provide excellent soft tissue detail and is useful for evaluating pelvic floor disorders. However, it is not considered a first-line imaging modality for urinary incontinence due to its high cost and limited availability. MRI might be considered in complex cases or when planning surgical intervention.

An intravenous pyelogram (IVP) is an outdated investigation that has largely been replaced by CT urography or ultrasound for most urological conditions. It is not indicated in the initial workup of urinary incontinence and exposes the patient to both radiation and intravenous contrast.

Urodynamic studies are not an imaging investigation per se, but rather a group of tests that evaluate the function of the lower urinary tract. While these studies can provide valuable information about bladder and urethral function, they are typically reserved for cases where the diagnosis remains unclear after initial evaluation or when conservative management has failed. Urodynamic studies are more invasive and time-consuming than ultrasound and are not considered a first-line investigation for uncomplicated urinary incontinence.

It is important to note that imaging studies should be considered after a thorough history, physical examination, and basic urinalysis in the workup of urinary incontinence. The choice of ultrasound as the initial imaging modality aligns with current guidelines for the evaluation of urinary incontinence in women, providing valuable information to guide further management while minimising patient risk and discomfort.
learningPoints: Ultrasound of the bladder and pelvis is the initial imaging investigation of choice for undifferentiated urinary incontinence in women.
Post-void residual volume measurement is an important component of the initial evaluation of urinary incontinence.
Imaging studies should be considered after a thorough history, physical examination, and basic urinalysis in the workup of urinary incontinence.
references: Hu JS, Pierre EF. Urinary Incontinence in Women: Evaluation and Management. Am Fam Physician. 2019;100(6):339-348.

Bodmer NS, Wirth C, Birkhäuser V, et al. Randomised Controlled Trials Assessing the Clinical Value of Urodynamic Studies: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. Eur Urol Open Sci. 2022;44:131-141. Published 2022 Sep 5. doi:10.1016/j.euros.2022.08.013
